Aneesh Raman, Boston University
Ph.D. @ DiSC Lab

  • About Me
  • Recent
  • Research
  • Resume/CV

About

I am a PhD student at Boston University and a member of the DiSC Lab, working with Prof. Manos Athanassoulis. My research focus is on Database systems - indexing and access methods, and my interests include high performance computing, data mining and machine learning. Prior to Boston University, I was at Purdue University Fort Wayne, where I graduated with highest distinction with a Bachelor's in Computer Science. There, I worked with Prof. Zesheng Chen on my senior capstone project.

Recent Updates

  • Dec 17, 2022 - Work on Indexing for Near-Sorted Data accepted @ IEEE ICDE 2023 Conference.
  • Sep 5, 2022 - Presented BoDS: A Benchmark on Data Sortedness at TPCTC (in VLDB 2022) in Sydney, Australia.
  • Jul 16, 2022 - Received NSF Travel Support Grant for VLDB 2022.
  • Jul 16, 2022 - Work on BoDS: A Benchmark on Data Sortedness accepted at TPCTC workshop @ VLDB 2022
  • Jun 1, 2022 - Recognized with the Outstanding Teaching Fellow Award at BU CS.
  • Apr 15, 2022 - Presented our work on "Indexing for Near-Sorted Data" @ CISE Graduate Student Workshop, BU

Research

SWARE: Indexing for Near-Sorted Data


Contributors: Aneesh Raman, Subhadeep Sarkar, Matthaios Olma, Manos Athanassoulis
    Resources:
  • Slides (CGSW)
  • Slides (CGSW)
  • Local PDF

Indexing in data systems can be perceived as the process of adding structure to the incoming, otherwise unsorted data. If the data ingestion order matches the indexed attribute order, the index ingestion cost is entirely redundant and can be avoided altogether. In this work, we identify sortedness as a resource that can accelerate index ingestion. We propose a new sortedness-aware (SWARE) design paradigm that pays less indexing cost for near-sorted data. When applied to a classical index like the B+-tree SWARE offers a 9× speedup for write intensive workloads and a 4× benefit in mixed workloads by exploiting data sortedness.

BoDS: A Benchmark on Data Sortedness


Contributors: Aneesh Raman, Konstantinos Karatsenidis, Subhadeep Sarkar, Matthaios Olma, Manos Athanassoulis
    Resources:
  • Slides (TPCTC-VLDB 2022)
  • Local PDF
  • Talk video

Data systems, and particularly, the underlying indexing data structures are designed to offer favorable ingestion (and query) performance for the two extremes of data sortedness, i.e., unsorted data or fully sorted data. However, in practice, data may arrive with an intermediate degree of pre-sortedness. In such cases, the intuition is that index construction should be cheaper when there is inherent data sortedness. Such sortedness-aware index designs lack from the literature. In fact, there is a need for a framework to explore how index designs may be able to exploit pre-existing sortedness during data ingestion to amortize the index construction cost. BoDS highlights the performance of data systems in terms of index construction and navigation costs when operating on data ingested with variable sortedness. Through our experiments we show the headroom for improvement in classical indexes, and we lay the groundwork for experimentation with sortedness-aware index designs.

Reducing Bloom Filter CPU Overhead in LSM-Trees on Modern Storage Devices

Contributors: Zichen Zhu, JuHyoung Mun, Aneesh Raman, Manos Athanassoulis
    Resources:
  • PDF
  • Youtube

Bloom filters (BFs) in LSM-trees are beneficial when there is significant performance difference between querying a BF and accessing data. This gap, however, is decreasing as modern storage devices (SSDs and NVMs) have increasingly lower latency. In this work, we propose to re-use hash calculations aggressively within and across BFs, as well as between different levels of the LSM-tree. The reduced CPU cost for queries using the hash sharing leads to 10% higher lookup performance in an LSM-tree stored in a state-of-the-art PCIe SSD, while for faster NVM devices, we observe performance gains of up to 40%.
